questions.question: The principle of supremacy of EU law was first established by the Court of Justice in which case?
questions.explanation: Costa v ENEL (Case 6/64) established the principle of supremacy. The Court held that by creating a Community of unlimited duration with its own institutions, legal personality and capacity, member states had limited their sovereign rights and created a body of law binding both themselves and their nationals. EU law takes precedence over conflicting national law.

questions.question: In Costa v ENEL [1964], Mr Costa challenged the nationalisation of ENEL (Italian electricity company). Why was the supremacy issue significant?

options.A: Because Costa argued that EEC Treaty provisions gave him property rights
options.B: Because Costa argued that Italian legislation nationalising ENEL was incompatible with EEC Treaty provisions, and the Italian court questioned whether later Italian law could override earlier EEC Treaty obligations
options.C: Because the Commission had brought infringement proceedings against Italy for the nationalisation
options.D: Because the nationalisation violated the free movement of capital under the EEC Treaty

questions.explanation: The issue arose because the Italian government argued that later Italian legislation (nationalising ENEL) superseded the earlier EEC Treaty under the rule lex posterior derogat legi priori (later law overrides earlier law). The Court of Justice rejected this, holding that EU law cannot be overridden by subsequent national law — its supremacy is inherent in the nature of the Treaty.

questions.question: The Simmenthal case [1978] (Case 106/77) expanded the supremacy doctrine by holding:

options.A: The Commission must bring infringement proceedings before national courts can disapply national law
options.B: National courts must immediately disapply conflicting national law without waiting for the national legislature or constitutional court to remove it
options.C: Member states must amend their constitutions to reflect EU law supremacy
options.D: Supremacy only applies to primary law, not to EU secondary legislation

questions.explanation: Simmenthal is the 'full effectiveness' case: the Court held that the principle of supremacy requires national courts to give full effect to EU law and, if necessary, to refuse of their own motion to apply any conflicting provision of national legislation — without waiting for it to be set aside by legislative or constitutional procedures. Immediate disapplication is the national court's obligation.

questions.question: The supremacy of EU law is NOT expressly stated in the Treaties. Where does it appear in the current treaty framework?

options.B: Declaration 17 annexed to the Final Act of the Lisbon Treaty, which refers to established Court of Justice case law

questions.explanation: Supremacy is not in the text of the TEU or TFEU (unlike the proposed Constitutional Treaty which would have included it expressly). Declaration 17 annexed to the Lisbon Treaty acknowledges the principle of supremacy as established in Court of Justice case law, citing Costa v ENEL. The declaration confirms the principle without making it a treaty article.

questions.question: What is the constitutional foundation for supremacy according to the Court of Justice in Costa v ENEL?

options.A: An international treaty principle that all international agreements override domestic law
options.B: The nature of the EEC Treaty itself — by creating an independent legal order to which member states transferred sovereign rights, member states permanently limited their legislative sovereignty
options.C: An express provision in the original Treaty of Rome requiring supremacy
options.D: The consent of member state constitutional courts at the time of accession

questions.explanation: The Court derived supremacy from the character of the EEC Treaty: member states had transferred sovereign rights to the Community, creating an independent legal order which could not be undermined by subsequent unilateral national measures without calling into question the legal basis of the Community itself. Supremacy is inherent in the Treaty's nature, not derived from an express provision.

questions.question: The 'ultra vires' review claim by some national constitutional courts (e.g., the German BVerfG) holds that:

options.A: EU law is not supreme over national law in any circumstances
options.B: National constitutional courts retain the right to review whether EU acts exceed the competences conferred by member states, even after Lisbon
options.C: The CJEU has no jurisdiction to develop EU law beyond the express Treaty text
options.D: National parliaments may override EU Regulations by absolute majority vote

questions.explanation: The BVerfG in Gauweiler [2016] and Weiss [2020] asserted Kompetenz-Kompetenz — the right of the national constitutional court to review whether EU institutions' acts are ultra vires (beyond the competences conferred). This creates a potential conflict with the CJEU's exclusive jurisdiction over EU law validity. The Weiss judgment (2020) actually exercised this review to find a CJEU ruling inadequate — a direct challenge to EU law supremacy.

questions.question: Supremacy means that, in the event of conflict between EU law and national law:

options.A: EU law is invalid if the national law was enacted first
options.B: National law must be disapplied to the extent of the conflict, whether the national rule predates or postdates the EU measure
options.C: The Commission decides which rule applies on a case-by-case basis
options.D: The conflict is resolved by the European Council by unanimous decision

questions.explanation: Supremacy is absolute and temporal — it applies regardless of whether the conflicting national law predates or postdates the EU measure (Costa: later Italian law could not override earlier EEC provisions). National law is not void — it continues to exist in national law — but national courts are obliged to disapply it in cases involving EU law.

questions.question: In R v Secretary of State for Transport ex parte Factortame [1990], the House of Lords and Court of Justice dealt with supremacy in what context?

options.A: Whether EU regulations on fishing quotas could be enforced against UK fishermen
options.B: Whether the UK courts had to grant interim injunctive relief against an Act of Parliament (the Merchant Shipping Act) in order to protect putative EU law rights, pending full hearing
options.C: Whether UK courts could award damages against the Crown for breach of EU law
options.D: Whether the UK's accession to the EEC impliedly repealed inconsistent earlier UK legislation

questions.explanation: Factortame is the UK's landmark supremacy case: the House of Lords, following CJEU guidance, granted an interim injunction suspending provisions of the Merchant Shipping Act 1988 — an Act of Parliament — because they were arguably incompatible with EU law. This was constitutionally dramatic: English courts had never previously 'set aside' an Act of Parliament. The CJEU confirmed national courts must set aside conflicting national law, even statutes.

questions.question: The Irish approach to EU law supremacy under the Constitution of Ireland is:

options.A: The Irish Constitution expressly states that EU law has absolute supremacy over all Irish law
options.B: Article 29.4.6° of the Constitution provides that no provision of the Constitution invalidates laws, acts or measures adopted by the EU, providing a constitutional basis for EU law supremacy in Ireland
options.C: EU law has supremacy only over legislation, not over the Irish Constitution
options.D: The Supreme Court has held that Irish common law overrides inconsistent EU secondary legislation

questions.explanation: Ireland amended its constitution (Third and subsequent Amendments) to accommodate EU membership. Article 29.4.6° Bunreacht na hÉireann provides that no provision of the Constitution invalidates laws enacted, acts done or measures adopted by the State that are necessitated by the obligations of EU membership, nor prevents the state from ratifying EU treaties. This constitutional accommodation makes EU supremacy constitutionally legitimate in Ireland.

questions.question: The principle of supremacy applies to which types of EU law?

options.A: Only primary law (the Treaties) — secondary law has supremacy only over conflicting ministerial regulations, not primary legislation
options.B: All EU law — primary and secondary — including Regulations, Directives, and CJEU decisions — all take precedence over conflicting national law of any status
options.C: Only Regulations (directly applicable acts) — Directives do not have supremacy
options.D: Only EU law in areas of exclusive competence — shared competence areas leave national law supreme

questions.explanation: Supremacy applies to the entirety of EU law — primary and secondary. A national act is disapplied whether it conflicts with the Treaty, a Regulation, a Directive (where directly effective), or a binding CJEU preliminary ruling. The national law's status in the domestic hierarchy (statute, constitutional provision, ministerial regulation) is irrelevant to the EU law supremacy analysis.

questions.question: The 'identity review' claimed by some national constitutional courts holds that:

options.A: The EU cannot require member states to identify their citizens for taxation purposes
options.B: EU law cannot require member states to violate the inviolable core ('constitutional identity') of their national constitution, even if it conflicts with EU obligations
options.C: National courts must identify and apply the closest equivalent EU rule when national law is unclear
options.D: The CJEU must verify the constitutional identity of each member state before applying EU law

questions.explanation: The identity review doctrine (developed by the BVerfG, French Conseil Constitutionnel, and others) asserts that the inviolable core of national constitutional identity — fundamental constitutional structures — cannot be overridden even by EU law. Article 4(2) TEU now expressly provides that the EU shall respect national identity inherent in fundamental structures of member states, providing a partial treaty basis for this claim.

questions.question: Why is the absence of an express supremacy clause in the Treaties (unlike the proposed Constitutional Treaty) constitutionally significant?

options.A: It means supremacy is not legally binding and can be challenged by member states
options.B: It means supremacy rests entirely on the Court of Justice's case law rather than an explicit treaty mandate — making it more vulnerable to national constitutional challenges
options.C: It means the European Parliament can amend the supremacy doctrine through the ordinary legislative procedure
options.D: It has no significance — supremacy has the same force regardless of whether it is expressed in the treaty text

questions.explanation: The absence of express supremacy in the Treaties means the doctrine rests on the Court's interpretation in Costa v ENEL and subsequent case law. National constitutional courts have used this to maintain that supremacy is a principle created by (and therefore conditional on) their own constitutional acceptance of EU membership — and is not absolute. The proposed Constitutional Treaty's explicit supremacy clause was one reason for French and Dutch 'no' votes in 2005 referendums.

questions.question: Under which circumstances, if any, may national courts refuse to apply a CJEU preliminary ruling that they find incompatible with national constitutional law?

options.A: Whenever the national court considers the ruling incorrectly decided
options.B: Never, as a matter of EU law — the national court is bound by the CJEU's preliminary ruling on EU law interpretation and must apply it
options.C: Always, if the national supreme court has issued a contrary ruling on the same point
options.D: Only if the ruling postdates national legislation implementing the relevant EU act

questions.explanation: As a matter of EU law, national courts are bound by CJEU preliminary rulings — the ruling is binding in the proceedings in which the reference was made and must be applied by the referring court. The BVerfG's Weiss judgment (2020), which found the CJEU's Weiss preliminary ruling inadequate and directed the Bundesbank not to comply, was a direct challenge to this principle — extraordinary and unprecedented in EU legal history.
